nativeRange.habitat: The Giant Gourami inhabits slow-moving and still waters across Southeast Asia, including large rivers, lakes, swamps, floodplains, rice paddies, and peat-swamp forests. It favors heavily vegetated, warm, shallow areas with soft substrates where it can reach the surface to breathe atmospheric air.

decorPreferences: driftwood for shelter and territory definition
robust or floating plants (water hyacinth, lotus) — soft plants will be eaten
open swimming areas essential at adult size
sturdy decor only — large fish will rearrange lightweight items

diet.notes: The Giant Gourami is a highly opportunistic omnivore. Juveniles readily accept high-quality pellets, cichlid sticks, earthworms, prawns, chopped fish, bloodworms, and blanched vegetables. Adults will consume almost anything organic, including large pellets, koi sticks, live or frozen fish, earthworms, snails, frogs, plant matter, fruit, and vegetables such as zucchini, peas, spinach, and banana. Although primarily herbivorous in the wild, aquarium specimens tend to shift readily toward more carnivorous feeding. Feed once or twice daily and avoid overfeeding, as adults produce considerable waste.

goodWith: large robust catfish (Pangasius, large Synodontis, Sailfin Pleco)
large cichlids of similar size (Oscar, Midas, Flowerhorn — with caution)
large Arowana (in very large systems only)
other Giant Gouramis (if raised together, space permitting)
avoidWith: similarly-sized but more aggressive species (risk of injury)
fish that cannot compete for food at feeding time

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Osphronemus goramy; adult specimen showing characteristic forehead hump and robust body profile.

summary: The Giant Gourami (Osphronemus goramy) is one of the largest labyrinth fish, reaching 70 cm and capable of exceeding 10 kg. An important food fish across Southeast Asia, it is best suited to indoor ponds rather than standard aquaria. Many individuals become strongly habituated to their keepers, accepting hand-feeding.
careNotes:
The Giant Gourami (Osphronemus goramy) is the largest member of the family Osphronemidae and one of the largest labyrinth fish (anabantoids) in the world, reaching 70 cm and over 10 kg in large aquaria and indoor ponds. It is frequently confused at point-of-sale with smaller gourami species — juveniles are sometimes sold as 'Giant Gouramis' when they are actually Banded Gouramis (Trichogaster fasciata) or other medium-sized species. Buyers should verify the scientific name before purchase.

As a labyrinth fish, O. goramy breathes atmospheric air via its labyrinth organ in addition to using its gills; access to the water surface is mandatory, and the tank must not be sealed. The species grows quickly — juveniles of 5–10 cm can reach 30+ cm within 12–18 months under good feeding, and tankmates that were initially compatible can rapidly become prey.

Adult males develop a pronounced nuchal hump (forehead bump) and thickened lips; females remain rounder and less pronounced in these features. Adults require a minimum tank of 1500 L (approximately 400 US gal) with a footprint of at least 300 cm × 90 cm, though indoor fish ponds of 3000–10000 L are strongly preferred by experienced keepers for long-term welfare. Most standard home aquaria are wholly unsuitable at adult size.

Water quality is paramount: Giant Gouramis are heavy waste producers and filtration must be sized accordingly (large external canister or sump, with turnover of at least 8–10× per hour). Regular large water changes (30–40% weekly) are essential. Temperature should be maintained at 24–28°C; the species tolerates a relatively wide pH range (6.5–8.0) but thrives in neutral to slightly alkaline conditions.

Juveniles can be kept in groups but adult males become increasingly territorial and may fight seriously; solitary keeping is recommended for adult males unless a very large system is available. Despite their size and semi-aggressive classification, Giant Gouramis can be surprisingly docile toward tankmates that are too large to eat, and many specimens become strongly habituated to their owners — accepting hand-feeding and approaching the glass reliably. This 'pet fish' personality is a major draw for dedicated keepers with appropriate facilities.
breedingNotes: The Giant Gourami is a bubble-nest spawner. The male constructs a large floating nest, sometimes incorporating plant material, and guards the eggs aggressively after spawning. Courtship involves the male wrapping around the female, and a single spawn can produce several hundred to several thousand eggs. The male provides sole parental care; the female should be separated after spawning to prevent aggression toward her. Eggs typically hatch within 24 to 36 hours, and fry become free-swimming after approximately three to four days. Initial foods for the fry include infusoria, micro-worms, and baby brine shrimp. Breeding in a standard home aquarium is uncommon given the tank size required; most successful captive breeding takes place in large outdoor ponds or commercial aquaculture facilities.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Osphronemus goramy
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 70 cm), water parameters (22–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 36–357 ppm), native range (Mekong, Chao Phraya, Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Java), diet, biotope, and husbandry notes including labyrinth organ requirement and minimum tank sizing.

additional.name: FishBase — Osphronemus goramy
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size (max 70 cm TL, common 45 cm), lifespan data, native countries (Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, Myanmar, Laos), taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), and omnivore diet classification. Confirmed food-fish status and widespread aquaculture use throughout SE Asia.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Giant Gourami Care
additional.notes: Trade-facing care guide; confirmed minimum tank size recommendation (300+ gal / ~1135 L for adults), feeding guidance (pellets, earthworms, vegetables), personality notes (pet-like behaviour, hand-feeding), and confusion with smaller species sold under the same common name.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Giant Gourami species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for adult morphology (nuchal hump development in males, thickened lips), breeding notes (bubble nest, male parental care, egg count), and indoor pond recommendations for long-term husbandry.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Giant Gourami (Osphronemus goramy)
additional.notes: Historical hobbyist reference; confirmed labyrinth organ air-breathing requirement, surface access necessity, heavy bioload and filtration sizing, and regional food-fish importance in SE Asia aquaculture.

fish.breedingNotes: The male constructs a large floating bubble nest and guards the eggs after spawning. A single spawn can produce several hundred to several thousand eggs. Successful captive breeding is rare in home aquaria due to the tank size required; most breeding occurs in outdoor ponds or commercial facilities. Newly hatched fry require infusoria and baby brine shrimp as initial foods.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Adults regularly reach 70 cm and require a minimum of 1500 L of water. Juveniles are frequently sold under the same common name as smaller species, which can obscure the animal's true adult size at point of purchase.
